problem_statement: The paper examines whether LLMs can reliably verify that code satisfies its natural-language requirements when used as automated code-review assistants or LLM judges, and shows they systematically misjudge correct code as non-conforming.
magnitude_evidence: On HumanEval, GPT-4o's RCRR declined from 52.4% with a direct prompt to merely 11.0% with the full three-step prompt (a 41.4 percentage-point drop); overall RCRR across models ranged only from 52% to 78%.
insufficiency_evidence: "LLMs frequently misclassify correct code implementations as either 'not satisfying requirements' or containing potential defects"; more complex prompting (explanations + proposed corrections) "leads to higher misjudgment rate", with GPT-4o dropping to 11.0% RCRR.
solution_mechanisms: Two-Phase Reflective Prompt (extract functional obligations, then audit code against each)
Behavioral Comparison Prompt (summarize spec behavior and code behavior separately, then compare point-by-point)
eliminate mandatory fix step / separate requirement comprehension from code auditing
